metadata.title: SAP-C02 Decision Patterns Knowledge Graph
metadata.modeling_note: Domains contain decision patterns; patterns carry IF/WHEN, THEN, NEVER, WHY attributes and link to concept/anti-pattern nodes.

nodes.label: Domain 1 — Design Solutions for Organizational Complexity
SCP Deny-List + Acquired Account Carve-Out
nodes.if_when: Root SCP blocks a resource type AND a new/acquired account needs temporary access to that blocked resource
nodes.then: Create a temporary OU (e.g. Onboarding ). Attach an Allow SCP for the carve-out to that OU. Move the account into it. After freeze period, move to Production OU — root SCP re-applies automatically.
never: IAM permission boundaries — cannot override SCPs
Adding a second Deny SCP to child OU — deny lists stack, they do not cancel
Condition keys on root SCP (e.g. aws:PrincipalAccount ) — per-account carve-outs must use OU structure
nodes.why: Effective permissions = intersection of ALL SCPs in the OU hierarchy. A child-OU Allow SCP carves out the exception while the root SCP remains unchanged. Authorization order: Organization SCPs → Resource-based policies → IAM policies → Permission boundaries → Session policies.

nodes.label: IAM permission boundaries — cannot override SCPs
Adding a second Deny SCP to child OU — deny lists stack, they do not cancel
Condition keys on root SCP (e.g. aws:PrincipalAccount ) — per-account carve-outs must use OU structure
SCP Evaluation Logic — Full Authorization Chain
nodes.if_when: Question asks whether an action will be allowed or denied given a combination of SCPs, IAM policies, and resource policies
nodes.then: Step Check Result if Deny 1 Explicit Deny anywhere (SCP, IAM, resource policy) DENY — final 2 Organization SCP — does it allow the action? DENY if not allowed 3 Resource-based policy (if cross-account) — explicit Allow? DENY if absent 4 IAM identity policy — explicit Allow? DENY if absent 5 Permission boundary (if exists) — within boundary? DENY if outside 6 Session policy (if AssumeRole) — within session scope? DENY if outside
never: Management account is exempt from SCPs — SCPs never apply to the management account
SCPs do not grant permissions — they only restrict what IAM policies CAN grant
NotAction in SCP = allow everything except listed actions (deny-list pattern)
Action in SCP with Allow = allow-list pattern — everything else is implicitly denied

nodes.label: Management account is exempt from SCPs — SCPs never apply to the management account
SCPs do not grant permissions — they only restrict what IAM policies CAN grant
NotAction in SCP = allow everything except listed actions (deny-list pattern)
Action in SCP with Allow = allow-list pattern — everything else is implicitly denied
Merging Two AWS Organizations Post-Acquisition
nodes.if_when: Merging two AWS Organizations after acquisition — move accounts, apply SCPs, preserve IAM resources, activate consolidated billing, unify SSO
nodes.then: Remove member accounts from acquired Org → management account sends Organization invitations → accounts accept → SCPs apply immediately on join → consolidated billing activates automatically → onboard employees to existing IAM Identity Center (Okta) by provisioning in IdP.
never: "AWS Support can merge Organizations" — this capability does not exist
Creating new accounts and migrating resources — violates no-re-provisioning requirement
Cross-org consolidated billing — not a supported feature, billing only works within one Org
nodes.why: Joining an Organization does not touch intra-account IAM resources. Consolidated billing and SCP enforcement activate the moment an account joins.

nodes.label: "AWS Support can merge Organizations" — this capability does not exist
Creating new accounts and migrating resources — violates no-re-provisioning requirement
Cross-org consolidated billing — not a supported feature, billing only works within one Org
Human Access to Production — No Persistent Credentials + External Auditor
nodes.if_when: Developers must use MFA, never persistent IAM credentials in Production; external auditor needs temporary read-only cross-account access for N days
nodes.then: IAM Identity Center (SAML + IdP, MFA enforced at IdP) — vends temporary session credentials per session. For external auditor: cross-account IAM role with trust policy to auditor's AWS account ID, ReadOnlyAccess , tag-based lifecycle automation (EventBridge + Lambda) to auto-delete after N days.
never: IAM users for external auditors — SCP blocks iam:CreateUser anyway
organizations:* on management account for external party — catastrophic blast radius
Per-account SAML federation bypassing Identity Center — N accounts × manual role management

nodes.label: IAM users for external auditors — SCP blocks iam:CreateUser anyway
organizations:* on management account for external party — catastrophic blast radius
Per-account SAML federation bypassing Identity Center — N accounts × manual role management
Centralized Network — TGW + GWLB + Route 53 Resolver + DX Gateway
nodes.if_when: 80+ account VPCs need centralized egress, centralized firewall inspection, shared on-prem DNS, shared Direct Connect, auto-enrollment for new accounts
nodes.then: Need Solution Centralized egress TGW spoke-rt default route → Egress VPC (NAT GW) Firewall inspection TGW two route tables + Gateway Load Balancer + Appliance Mode on Inspection VPC attachment Hybrid DNS Route 53 Resolver Inbound/Outbound Endpoints in central VPC → Rules shared via AWS RAM Shared Direct Connect Direct Connect Gateway attached to TGW (one DX shared across all VPCs) Auto-enrollment TGW shared via RAM to Org + auto-accept enabled
never: VPC Peering for hub-and-spoke — non-transitive, n×(n-1)/2 connections at scale
Per-account Direct Connect VIFs — expensive, unscalable
Omit Appliance Mode — causes asymmetric routing, stateful firewall breaks across AZs

nodes.label: VPC Peering for hub-and-spoke — non-transitive, n×(n-1)/2 connections at scale
Per-account Direct Connect VIFs — expensive, unscalable
Omit Appliance Mode — causes asymmetric routing, stateful firewall breaks across AZs
Organization-Wide Security Controls — Auto-Enrollment Within 5 Minutes
nodes.if_when: All accounts need GuardDuty, Security Hub, CloudTrail — new accounts auto-inherit within minutes, members cannot opt out, tamper-proof audit logs 7 years
nodes.then: Requirement Service All-account CloudTrail + auto-enroll CloudTrail Organization Trail (single trail, all current + future accounts) Immutable logs S3 + Object Lock COMPLIANCE mode (even root cannot delete within retention) SQL queries on CloudTrail CloudTrail Lake (no Athena/Glue setup required) GuardDuty auto-enroll GuardDuty delegated admin + AutoEnable: ALL Security Hub aggregate Security Hub delegated admin + auto-enable Prevent opt-out SCP: Deny guardduty:DeleteDetector , cloudtrail:StopLogging , organizations:LeaveOrganization
never: Lambda + EventBridge for new account auto-enrollment — custom code, 15-min CloudTrail delivery delay
Config Aggregator for GuardDuty findings — Config tracks resource config, not threat findings
CloudFormation StackSets — 5-15 min deployment delay per account batch

nodes.label: Lambda + EventBridge for new account auto-enrollment — custom code, 15-min CloudTrail delivery delay
Config Aggregator for GuardDuty findings — Config tracks resource config, not threat findings
CloudFormation StackSets — 5-15 min deployment delay per account batch
Config Conformance Packs — Continuous Compliance + Auto-Remediation at Scale
nodes.if_when: Enforce compliance rules (S3 versioning, approved AMIs, VPC Flow Logs, encryption) across all accounts, auto-remediate within minutes, unified dashboard
nodes.then: AWS Config Organization Conformance Pack (deploys Config rules + SSM Automation remediation to all accounts from one template). Change-triggered evaluation fires within 2 minutes of resource creation. Security Hub aggregates compliance scores. Approved AMIs shared via EC2 Image Builder + AWS RAM .
never: CloudTrail-triggered Lambda — up to 15-minute delivery delay for management events
Control Tower for existing account remediation — designed for new account vending, not retroactive
Hourly Lambda scans — cannot meet 2-minute detection requirement

nodes.label: CloudTrail-triggered Lambda — up to 15-minute delivery delay for management events
Control Tower for existing account remediation — designed for new account vending, not retroactive
Hourly Lambda scans — cannot meet 2-minute detection requirement
AWS Control Tower — Landing Zone, Guardrails, Account Factory
nodes.if_when: Need to set up a new multi-account AWS environment with governance baseline, or vend new accounts with pre-configured controls automatically
nodes.then: Control Tower Component What it Does Landing Zone Pre-built multi-account structure (Management, Log Archive, Audit accounts) Account Factory Self-service account vending with pre-applied baselines (Config, CloudTrail, SSO) Preventive guardrails SCPs that prevent policy violations (e.g. disabling CloudTrail) Detective guardrails Config rules that detect non-compliance (e.g. S3 public access enabled) Proactive guardrails CloudFormation hooks that block non-compliant resources before creation
never: Control Tower for retroactive compliance in existing accounts — it manages accounts it vends; existing accounts need enrollment and may drift
Control Tower for 5-minute new account enrollment SLA — Account Factory takes longer; use delegated admin auto-enable for security services

nodes.label: Control Tower for retroactive compliance in existing accounts — it manages accounts it vends; existing accounts need enrollment and may drift
Control Tower for 5-minute new account enrollment SLA — Account Factory takes longer; use delegated admin auto-enable for security services
AWS RAM — Resource Sharing Across Accounts and Organizations
nodes.if_when: Need to share AWS resources across accounts without duplicating them — subnets, TGW, Route 53 Resolver rules, License Manager, EC2 Image Builder AMIs
nodes.then: AWS Resource Access Manager (RAM) — share resources to specific accounts, OUs, or the entire Organization. Key shareable resources: Resource Use Case VPC Subnets Shared VPC — central networking team owns subnets, spoke accounts deploy into them Transit Gateway Shared TGW across all accounts — spoke VPCs attach from their own accounts Route 53 Resolver Rules Central DNS rules shared to all spoke accounts EC2 Image Builder AMIs Hardened golden AMIs available to all accounts License Manager Shared software license pools across accounts
never: Recreating resources in each account (e.g. one TGW per account) — defeats the purpose of shared services
RAM sharing to individual accounts when the entire Org needs it — share at Organization level

nodes.label: Recreating resources in each account (e.g. one TGW per account) — defeats the purpose of shared services
RAM sharing to individual accounts when the entire Org needs it — share at Organization level
S3 Data Classification + Access Control — Macie + SCPs + Object Lock
nodes.if_when: Auto-classify S3 objects (PII, PHI, CONFIDENTIAL) on upload, restrict access by classification, enforce via SCP (account admins cannot override), immutable audit logs, scan existing objects
nodes.then: Amazon Macie (delegated admin from Security account) auto-detects PII/PHI → EventBridge rule → Lambda applies S3 Object Tags ( classification=PII ). SCP with s3:ExistingObjectTag condition : Deny s3:GetObject where tag=PII unless principal matches approved role ARN. S3 Object Lock COMPLIANCE on audit bucket (even root cannot delete). Macie scheduled discovery jobs for existing objects.
never: Amazon Rekognition for PII detection — Rekognition classifies images/video only
Bucket policies alone — account admins can override, SCP is required for org-level enforcement
S3 Object Lock GOVERNANCE mode for immutability — GOVERNANCE can be overridden by users with s3:BypassGovernanceRetention . COMPLIANCE mode = truly immutable

nodes.label: Amazon Rekognition for PII detection — Rekognition classifies images/video only
Bucket policies alone — account admins can override, SCP is required for org-level enforcement
S3 Object Lock GOVERNANCE mode for immutability — GOVERNANCE can be overridden by users with s3:BypassGovernanceRetention . COMPLIANCE mode = truly immutable
Multi-Account Cost Allocation — CUR, Cost Categories, Budgets, Savings Plans Sharing
nodes.if_when: Need per-BU showback, shared cost allocation, SQL-queryable line-item cost data, automated budget alerts to different contacts, RI/SP sharing
nodes.then: Requirement Service Line-item SQL cost queries CUR → S3 → Glue → Athena Shared service cost allocation Cost Categories split charge rules Per-BU showback Cost Allocation Tags + Cost Explorer Per-BU budget alerts to different contacts AWS Budgets → SNS → Lambda → DynamoDB lookup → SES RI/SP sharing across accounts Consolidated billing — automatic , no config needed
never: QuickSight with Cost Explorer as data source — Cost Explorer is pre-aggregated, not line-item granularity. CUR → Athena gives full line-item detail

nodes.label: QuickSight with Cost Explorer as data source — Cost Explorer is pre-aggregated, not line-item granularity. CUR → Athena gives full line-item detail
Hybrid DNS — On-Premises ↔ AWS Name Resolution
nodes.if_when: EC2 instances in VPC need to resolve on-premises hostnames (e.g. server.corp.internal ), OR on-premises servers need to resolve AWS private hosted zone names
nodes.then: Direction Solution VPC → On-premises (forward queries for corp.internal ) Route 53 Resolver Outbound Endpoint + Forwarding Rule → on-prem DNS IP On-premises → VPC (resolve *.aws.corp private hosted zone) Route 53 Resolver Inbound Endpoint — on-prem DNS forwards to Inbound Endpoint IP Share rules across all accounts Share Resolver Rules via AWS RAM to Organization
never: Deploy DNS servers on EC2 in each account — operational overhead, not scalable
Confuse Inbound vs Outbound: Inbound = traffic comes IN from on-prem to AWS. Outbound = queries go OUT from AWS to on-prem

nodes.label: Deploy DNS servers on EC2 in each account — operational overhead, not scalable
Confuse Inbound vs Outbound: Inbound = traffic comes IN from on-prem to AWS. Outbound = queries go OUT from AWS to on-prem
Direct Connect — Architecture, Redundancy, and Failover Patterns
nodes.if_when: Need dedicated, private, consistent-bandwidth connectivity from on-premises to AWS — or questions about DX redundancy and failover to VPN
nodes.then: Pattern Components Single VPC connectivity DX Connection → Private VIF → Virtual Private Gateway (VGW) Multiple VPCs via one DX DX Connection → Direct Connect Gateway → Transit Gateway or multiple VGWs High availability (SLA 99.99%) Two DX connections from two different DX locations → two VIFs DX + VPN failover BGP metrics: DX = lower MED (preferred). VPN = higher MED (backup). Automatic failover. Routing control BGP community tags control route propagation. AS_PATH prepending for traffic engineering.
never: Public VIF for private VPC resources — Public VIF is for AWS public endpoints (S3, DynamoDB APIs)
Single DX connection for mission-critical — single DX = single point of failure. Always two connections for HA

nodes.label: Public VIF for private VPC resources — Public VIF is for AWS public endpoints (S3, DynamoDB APIs)
Single DX connection for mission-critical — single DX = single point of failure. Always two connections for HA
Streaming Architecture Decision — Kinesis vs MSK vs EventBridge vs SQS
nodes.if_when: Need to choose between Kinesis, MSK, EventBridge, and SQS for event/message processing
nodes.then: Service Use When Key Limit Kinesis Data Streams High-throughput ordered streaming, replay, fan-out, Flink/Lambda consumers 1MB/s or 1K records/s per shard Amazon MSK (Kafka) Existing Kafka workloads, Kafka Streams, MirrorMaker, exactly-once with Transactions API No managed cross-region replication EventBridge Event routing between AWS services, SaaS integrations, low-volume event-driven triggers ~10K events/sec default per bus SQS Standard Decoupling, at-least-once, high-throughput queue, dead-letter, long polling Unlimited throughput, no ordering SQS FIFO Exactly-once, strict ordering within message group 3K msg/sec with batching SNS Fan-out to multiple subscribers (SQS, Lambda, HTTP, email) No replay, fire-and-forget
never: High throughput (>10K/sec) → Kinesis or MSK . Routing/orchestration → EventBridge . Decoupling workers → SQS . Fan-out → SNS → SQS

nodes.label: High throughput (>10K/sec) → Kinesis or MSK . Routing/orchestration → EventBridge . Decoupling workers → SQS . Fan-out → SNS → SQS
High-Throughput Streaming + Exactly-Once + Sub-200ms Latency
nodes.if_when: 500K+ events/sec ingestion, exactly-once processing, enrichment updatable without downtime, results available <200ms, multi-tenant isolation
nodes.then: Kinesis Data Streams (partition by high-cardinality key) → Amazon Managed Apache Flink (exactly-once via S3 checkpointing; blue/green JAR swap = zero-downtime update) → DynamoDB on-demand (single-digit ms reads) → API Gateway + Lambda authorizer
never: One Kinesis stream per tenant — thousands of streams, unmanageable at scale
EventBridge for 500K/sec — default ~10K/sec limit
Athena for <200ms reads — batch query engine
MSK if team explicitly rejected Kafka — honor the constraint
nodes.why: Flink exactly-once = checkpoint to S3. Flink blue/green = application snapshot → new JAR → restore from snapshot. No data loss, no duplication.

nodes.label: One Kinesis stream per tenant — thousands of streams, unmanageable at scale
Athena for <200ms reads — batch query engine
MSK if team explicitly rejected Kafka — honor the constraint
Global Leaderboard — Sub-10ms Reads, 500K Writes/sec, RPO=0
nodes.if_when: Global leaderboard: sub-10ms reads, 500K writes/sec, RPO=0, RTO<30s, no pre-provisioning, 90-day audit
nodes.then: DynamoDB Global Tables on-demand (durable score writes, active-active, RPO=0, LWW conflict resolution) → ElastiCache Redis Global Datastore sorted sets ZADD/ZRANGE (sub-1ms leaderboard reads per region) → DynamoDB Streams → EventBridge Pipes → Kinesis Firehose → S3 (90-day audit)
never: DynamoDB GSI for leaderboard ranking — GSIs are not globally sorted across all partitions
Redis Global Datastore alone — async replication ≠ RPO=0, no durable backup on cluster restart
Aurora Global Database for 500K writes/sec — single writer bottleneck

nodes.label: DynamoDB GSI for leaderboard ranking — GSIs are not globally sorted across all partitions
Redis Global Datastore alone — async replication ≠ RPO=0, no durable backup on cluster restart
Aurora Global Database for 500K writes/sec — single writer bottleneck
HIPAA IoT at Scale — RPO=0, 500ms Alert, 7-Year Retention, BAA Required
nodes.if_when: HIPAA IoT ingestion at millions of devices, anomaly alert <500ms, RPO=0 on region failure, 7-year retention, CMK encryption, all services need BAA
nodes.then: IoT Core → Kinesis Data Streams in TWO regions simultaneously (dual-write producers = RPO=0) → Managed Flink (500ms tumbling window) → DynamoDB Global Tables KMS CMK → S3 + Glacier Deep Archive lifecycle (7-year) → Route 53 ARC (RTO <1min)
never: Single-region Kinesis — RPO≠0
ElastiCache Redis — NOT HIPAA BAA eligible
Aurora Global DB — replication lag ~1s ≠ RPO=0
EventBridge for 2M device throughput — ~10K/sec limit
nodes.why: RPO=0 requires dual-region producers . Any replication-after-the-fact approach always has lag → RPO > 0.

nodes.label: ElastiCache Redis — NOT HIPAA BAA eligible
Aurora Global DB — replication lag ~1s ≠ RPO=0
SageMaker Endpoint Types — Real-Time vs Serverless vs Async vs Batch
nodes.if_when: Need to choose the right SageMaker inference endpoint type based on latency, cost, and traffic pattern
nodes.then: Endpoint Type Latency Use When Cost Model Real-Time Milliseconds Fraud detection, recommendations, <1s SLA Per instance-hour (always on) Serverless Seconds (cold start) <10 req/day, cost-sensitive, spiky traffic Per invocation + compute time Asynchronous Minutes Large payloads (>6MB), long processing, queued batch Per instance-hour (while processing) Batch Transform Hours Offline bulk inference on S3 datasets Per instance-hour (job duration)
never: Async Inference ≠ real-time. If the question says <100ms or <200ms scoring SLA → Real-Time endpoint . Async is for large payloads where response can wait minutes

nodes.label: Async Inference ≠ real-time. If the question says <100ms or <200ms scoring SLA → Real-Time endpoint . Async is for large payloads where response can wait minutes
Active-Active Global API — 100K RPS, Instant Region Removal (No DNS TTL)
nodes.if_when: Multi-region active-active API, 100K RPS, remove region from rotation instantly (no DNS TTL delay), sub-50ms session access globally
nodes.then: AWS Global Accelerator (anycast IPs, AWS backbone routing, endpoint weight=0 = instant removal) → regional ALB + ECS Fargate → DynamoDB Global Tables on-demand → ElastiCache Global Datastore (sub-ms regional session reads)
never: Route 53 latency-based routing for instant removal — DNS TTL minimum 60s
CloudFront + Lambda@Edge for dynamic API logic — Lambda@Edge max 30s, no VPC access
S3 for session state — 10-50ms read latency
nodes.why: Global Accelerator weight=0 = instant . Route 53 record change = DNS TTL propagation. Use GA when instant regional removal or health-check based routing over AWS backbone is required.

nodes.label: Route 53 latency-based routing for instant removal — DNS TTL minimum 60s
CloudFront + Lambda@Edge for dynamic API logic — Lambda@Edge max 30s, no VPC access
S3 for session state — 10-50ms read latency
Step Functions Standard vs Express — The Critical Trap
nodes.if_when: Need to orchestrate a multi-step workflow with retries, parallel branches, compensating transactions, or business analyst visibility
nodes.then: Attribute Standard Workflow Express Workflow Max duration 1 year 5 minutes Execution semantics Exactly-once At-least-once Execution history Full history in console CloudWatch Logs only Pricing Per state transition Per execution duration + count Use case Order processing, human approval, DR workflows, anything >5min High-volume, short IoT/streaming pipelines Use Parallel state for concurrent branches. Catch + Compensation states for Saga pattern. WaitForTaskToken for human approval or async callbacks.
never: Express for workflows lasting longer than 5 minutes — this is the #1 Step Functions trap on the exam. If duration can exceed 5 min → Standard, always

nodes.label: Express for workflows lasting longer than 5 minutes — this is the #1 Step Functions trap on the exam. If duration can exceed 5 min → Standard, always
Database Selection — Right Service for the Right Workload
nodes.if_when: Need to select the right AWS database for a given access pattern, data model, and SLA
nodes.then: Database Use When Aurora MySQL/PostgreSQL Relational OLTP, ACID, complex joins, stored procedures, lift-and-shift from MySQL/Oracle/SQL Server Aurora Global Database Multi-region active-read, RPO <1s, RTO <1min with managed failover Aurora Serverless v2 Variable/unpredictable workload, pay-per-ACU, scales in <1s, no idle cost at 0.5 ACU min DynamoDB Single-table design, key-value/document, <10ms at any scale, 500K+ writes/sec, no joins DynamoDB Global Tables Active-active multi-region, RPO=0, LWW conflict resolution, <1s replication ElastiCache Redis Sub-ms reads, session state, leaderboards (sorted sets), pub/sub, caching layer in front of RDS/DynamoDB Amazon Timestream Time-series data (IoT sensor data, metrics, log data with timestamps) Amazon QLDB Immutable, cryptographically verifiable ledger (financial audit, supply chain tracking) Amazon OpenSearch Full-text search, log analytics, complex aggregations across large datasets Amazon Redshift Analytical OLAP queries on structured data, multi-TB to PB datasets, columnar storage

nodes.label: Serverless Compute Selection — Lambda vs Fargate vs App Runner
nodes.if_when: Need to choose between Lambda, ECS Fargate, or App Runner for a serverless or containerized workload
nodes.then: Service Use When Key Limits Lambda Event-driven, stateless, <15min execution, scale-to-zero, pay-per-invocation 15min timeout, 10GB memory, 10GB /tmp, no GPU ECS Fargate Containerized, >15min tasks, custom runtimes, GPU inference, persistent connections 4 vCPU / 30GB per task (standard), scale-to-zero possible App Runner HTTP/HTTPS web apps and APIs from container or source code, no infrastructure management Automatic scaling, built-in load balancing, no VPC required
never: Lambda for: GPU inference (no GPU support), >15min processing, persistent TCP connections, large binary processing >10GB. Use Fargate instead

nodes.label: Lambda for: GPU inference (no GPU support), >15min processing, persistent TCP connections, large binary processing >10GB. Use Fargate instead
Multi-Tenant SaaS — Physical Data Isolation + 60-Second Tenant Onboarding
nodes.if_when: SaaS with 100K tenants, physical isolation (bug-proof), per-tenant retention, cross-tenant analytics for platform owner only, 60-second automated onboarding
nodes.then: Per-tenant S3 bucket + unique KMS CMK (physical crypto isolation). Per-tenant DynamoDB table (table-level IAM policy). Step Functions + Lambda onboarding (~15-30s to provision all resources). Per-bucket S3 Lifecycle for tenant-specific retention. Glue Data Catalog + Athena for cross-tenant analytics (platform owner role only).
never: One account per tenant — account creation takes 5-15min, violates 60s SLA
Shared DynamoDB table with tenant_id — application-layer filter only, bug = data leak
Shared S3 bucket with prefix — same KMS key = not physical isolation
nodes.why: Strongest → weakest: Separate AWS account → Separate resource + separate KMS key → Separate prefix → Row-level filter (application only)

nodes.label: One account per tenant — account creation takes 5-15min, violates 60s SLA
Shared DynamoDB table with tenant_id — application-layer filter only, bug = data leak
Shared S3 bucket with prefix — same KMS key = not physical isolation
Live Video Streaming — Ultra-Low Latency, ABR, DVR, Viral Spikes
nodes.if_when: Live sports streaming: <5s latency, ABR, 30-min DVR, 100K+ concurrent viewers, handles 10× viral spikes in 30 seconds, global audience
nodes.then: AWS Elemental MediaLive (managed live encoder, multiple bitrate renditions, input redundancy) → AWS Elemental MediaPackage (ABR packaging HLS/DASH/CMAF, Low-Latency HLS = 3-5s glass-to-glass, native DVR window up to 72h) → CloudFront + Origin Shield (edge cache absorbs spikes)
never: Kinesis Video Streams for live viewer delivery — designed for ML/analytics ingest, not HLS/DASH output
EC2 Auto Scaling as streaming servers — 60-90s launch time cannot handle 30s spikes
Lambda for real-time transcoding — no multi-bitrate encoding capability
nodes.why: Spike → CloudFront cache hit rate increases → origin load does NOT scale proportionally. CloudFront is the auto-scaler for CDN traffic.

nodes.label: Kinesis Video Streams for live viewer delivery — designed for ML/analytics ingest, not HLS/DASH output
EC2 Auto Scaling as streaming servers — 60-90s launch time cannot handle 30s spikes
Lambda for real-time transcoding — no multi-bitrate encoding capability
CloudFront — Edge Caching, OAC, Lambda@Edge, Functions, Security
nodes.if_when: Need to reduce origin load, accelerate static/dynamic content globally, enforce security at edge, or customize request/response
nodes.then: Feature Use When Origin Access Control (OAC) Restrict S3 access to CloudFront only (replaces legacy OAI). Eliminates direct S3 URL access. Lambda@Edge Complex request manipulation, A/B testing, JWT validation at edge. Max 30s (viewer) / 5s (origin). CloudFront Functions Lightweight URL rewrites, header manipulation, <1ms execution. Cheaper than Lambda@Edge. Origin Shield Additional caching layer between edge PoPs and origin. Reduces origin load by 60-80% during spikes. WAF integration AWS WAF on CloudFront distribution = global DDoS + application-layer protection at edge Price Class 100 = NA + Europe only (cheapest). 200 = + Asia/ME/Africa. All = all PoPs (most expensive).
never: Lambda@Edge for complex dynamic API logic requiring VPC connectivity — Lambda@Edge cannot be in a VPC. Use regional Lambda + API Gateway instead

nodes.label: Lambda@Edge for complex dynamic API logic requiring VPC connectivity — Lambda@Edge cannot be in a VPC. Use regional Lambda + API Gateway instead
API Gateway — REST vs HTTP vs WebSocket Selection
nodes.if_when: Need to expose APIs to clients — choose between REST, HTTP, or WebSocket API
nodes.then: Type Cost Use When Missing vs REST REST API $3.50/M calls API keys, usage plans, request validation, caching, edge-optimized, WAF — HTTP API $1.00/M calls (71% cheaper) Lambda/HTTP proxy, JWT authorizers, CORS, custom domains No API keys, no caching, no request transformation, no WAF direct integration WebSocket API $1.00/M messages Real-time bidirectional (chat, live updates, collaborative apps) —
never: HTTP API when you need: API keys + usage plans, response caching, request/response mapping templates, WAF integration, or edge-optimized endpoint. These features require REST API

nodes.label: HTTP API when you need: API keys + usage plans, response caching, request/response mapping templates, WAF integration, or edge-optimized endpoint. These features require REST API
Migration Tool Selection — MGN vs DMS vs DataSync vs Snowball vs SCT
nodes.if_when: Need to select the right migration tool based on workload type, data volume, and available bandwidth
nodes.then: Tool Use For Key Capability AWS MGN VM/physical server lift-and-shift to EC2 Continuous block-level replication, sub-hour cutover AWS DMS + CDC Database migration with minimal downtime Ongoing replication until cutover, heterogeneous engines AWS SCT Schema + stored procedure conversion (Oracle → PG, SQL Server → Aurora) Assessment report + auto-conversion + action items AWS DataSync NFS/SMB/HDFS/S3 → S3/EFS/FSx, large file transfers Bandwidth throttle schedules, end-to-end checksum, parallel threads Snowball Edge Bulk data transfer when bandwidth is insufficient for timeline 100TB usable/device, offline, KMS encryption AWS Backup Centralized backup policy across EC2, RDS, DynamoDB, EFS, S3 Backup plans, PITR, cross-account/region backup
never: AWS SMS — deprecated . Always use MGN
DMS for SAP HANA — DMS does not support HANA as source/target
Snowball when network bandwidth makes transfer feasible in time — DataSync is faster and supports live CDC
Transfer Family (SFTP) for bulk NAS migration — designed for file transfer workflows, not bulk migration

nodes.label: AWS SMS — deprecated . Always use MGN
DMS for SAP HANA — DMS does not support HANA as source/target
Snowball when network bandwidth makes transfer feasible in time — DataSync is faster and supports live CDC
Transfer Family (SFTP) for bulk NAS migration — designed for file transfer workflows, not bulk migration
Bandwidth Rule — When to Use Snowball vs DataSync
nodes.if_when: Need to transfer large datasets to AWS — determining whether network transfer or physical shipment is faster
nodes.then: Available Bandwidth Data Size Transfer Time Decision 1 Gbps 10TB ~22 hours DataSync over network 1 Gbps 200TB ~18 days DataSync if timeline allows, else Snowball 200 Mbps 10PB ~4.6 YEARS Snowball Edge (100 devices) 500 Mbps 8TB NFS ~35 hours DataSync over internet Formula: Transfer time (hours) = Data size (bytes) / (Bandwidth (bits/s) / 8) / 3600. Compare to timeline. If DataSync takes longer than timeline → Snowball + DataSync for delta.
never: Snowball when DataSync is faster — Snowball adds 7-10 days shipping round-trip. DataSync + network is always preferred when bandwidth makes it feasible

nodes.label: Snowball when DataSync is faster — Snowball adds 7-10 days shipping round-trip. DataSync + network is always preferred when bandwidth makes it feasible
VMware Lift-and-Shift — MGN + DataSync (NFS→EFS) + DMS CDC
nodes.if_when: 40 VMware VMs, shared NFS mount, Oracle database, <2hr downtime, 4hr rollback SLA, centrally tracked
nodes.then: AWS MGN (continuous block-level replication, delta lag = seconds by cutover day) + DataSync NFS → Amazon EFS (drop-in NFS replacement, parallel transfer, checksum) + DMS Full Load + CDC for Oracle (zero data loss) → cutover: stop writes, CDC lag <60s, flip DNS → Migration Hub for tracking → Rollback: MGN agents on-prem, repoint DNS
never: S3 File Gateway for shared NFS — caching latency unsuitable for concurrent NFS writes
DMS Full Load only — 3TB at 500Mbps = 13+ hours, violates 2hr window
Snowball for 8TB NFS — 7-10 day shipping, no live delta sync
nodes.why: EFS = drop-in NFS replacement (Linux). FSx for Windows = SMB + AD integrated (Windows). Storage Gateway File GW = NFS/SMB interface to S3, caching, lower concurrency performance.

nodes.label: S3 File Gateway for shared NFS — caching latency unsuitable for concurrent NFS writes
DMS Full Load only — 3TB at 500Mbps = 13+ hours, violates 2hr window
Snowball for 8TB NFS — 7-10 day shipping, no live delta sync
Oracle → Aurora PostgreSQL Migration — SCT + DMS + Lambda for PL/SQL
nodes.if_when: Migrating Oracle (large, PL/SQL packages) to Aurora PostgreSQL, <1hr downtime, eliminate Oracle licensing, 1Gbps DX available
nodes.then: AWS SCT (assess + auto-convert DDL/PL/SQL; generates complexity report) → DMS Full Load + CDC over Direct Connect → For unconvertible PL/SQL: replace with Lambda functions called via aws_lambda Aurora extension → Cutover when CDC lag <60 seconds
never: Snowball when 1Gbps DX available — DX is faster, Snowball adds 2-3 week shipping
DMS Full Load only (no CDC) — requires full maintenance window (hours)
RDS for Oracle as target — retains Oracle licensing (violates cost requirement)
"Manually refactor all PL/SQL" — missing the Lambda accelerator pattern for unconverted code

nodes.label: Snowball when 1Gbps DX available — DX is faster, Snowball adds 2-3 week shipping
DMS Full Load only (no CDC) — requires full maintenance window (hours)
RDS for Oracle as target — retains Oracle licensing (violates cost requirement)
"Manually refactor all PL/SQL" — missing the Lambda accelerator pattern for unconverted code
Windows .NET App — No Code Changes, AD Auth, SMB, MSMQ, SQL Server
nodes.if_when: Migrating .NET Framework app: Windows Authentication, SMB file share, MSMQ, SQL Server — NO code changes allowed
nodes.then: Requirement AWS Service Windows app, no code changes EC2 Windows (lift-and-shift via MGN) Active Directory / Windows Auth AWS Managed Microsoft AD SMB file share Amazon FSx for Windows File Server (native SMB, AD-integrated) MSMQ (no code changes) MSMQ on EC2 (co-located with app — migrating to SQS requires code changes) SQL Server RDS for SQL Server (same engine) via DMS CDC
never: MSMQ → SQS without code changes — impossible. MSMQ uses COM API; SQS uses SDK/HTTP
EFS for SMB — EFS is NFS only. Windows needs FSx for Windows
Aurora for SQL Server target — changes engine, violates SQL Server constraint

EFS for SMB — EFS is NFS only. Windows needs FSx for Windows

Migration Discovery Toolchain — ADS Agent, Migration Evaluator, Migration Hub
nodes.if_when: Migrating 500 applications: need to classify by 6Rs, auto-discover dependencies, track centrally, throttle file transfers, stakeholder visibility
nodes.then: Need Tool Why Process-level dependency mapping ADS Discovery Agent Captures actual network connections between processes. Agentless Connector = VMware-only, VM-level only. Portfolio assessment + business case Migration Evaluator Automated right-sizing, TCO analysis, wave recommendations 6R classification + central tracking AWS Migration Hub Single pane across all migration tools and accounts Throttled file transfer with integrity DataSync with --bandwidth-limit Scheduled throttle, automatic checksum, parallel
never: ADS Discovery Connector for process-level dependencies — VMware-only, VM metadata only
SSM Inventory for network dependency mapping — discovers installed software, not connections
Transfer Family for bulk NAS migration — SFTP protocol, not bulk data movement

Database Migration Target Mapping — Oracle, MySQL, SQL Server, MongoDB, PostgreSQL
nodes.if_when: Need to select the optimal AWS target database for each source engine
nodes.then: Source Engine Optimal AWS Target Tool Oracle (OLTP) Aurora PostgreSQL SCT + DMS CDC MySQL Aurora MySQL DMS CDC (homogeneous, no SCT needed) SQL Server (preserve T-SQL/Agent Jobs) RDS for SQL Server DMS CDC MongoDB (time-series data) Amazon Timestream DMS (MongoDB supported as source) MongoDB (document) Amazon DocumentDB DMS PostgreSQL Aurora PostgreSQL DMS CDC (homogeneous)
never: MySQL → DynamoDB — complete re-architecture (relational → NoSQL)
MongoDB time-series → DocumentDB — DocumentDB not optimized for time-series; use Timestream
All databases → DynamoDB — requires full application redesign
DMS for SAP HANA — not supported

nodes.label: MongoDB time-series → DocumentDB — DocumentDB not optimized for time-series; use Timestream
All databases → DynamoDB — requires full application redesign
DMS for SAP HANA — not supported
Storage Gateway — File Gateway vs Volume Gateway vs Tape Gateway
nodes.if_when: On-premises applications need to access AWS storage (S3, EBS snapshots, Glacier) without changing their protocols
nodes.then: Gateway Type Protocol Backend Use When File Gateway NFS / SMB Amazon S3 Hybrid NFS/SMB access to S3. Files stored as S3 objects. Cache for frequently accessed files. Volume Gateway (Cached) iSCSI S3 + local cache Block storage with primary data in S3, frequently accessed data cached locally Volume Gateway (Stored) iSCSI Local + S3 snapshots All data stored locally, asynchronous snapshots to S3 for backup Tape Gateway iSCSI VTL S3 + Glacier Replace physical tape infrastructure with virtual tapes archived to Glacier
never: File Gateway for high-concurrency, high-performance NFS workloads — caching latency and throughput limits make it unsuitable. For production NFS → use Amazon EFS. For production SMB → use FSx for Windows

nodes.label: File Gateway for high-concurrency, high-performance NFS workloads — caching latency and throughput limits make it unsuitable. For production NFS → use Amazon EFS. For production SMB → use FSx for Windows
Hybrid Architecture — Storage Gateway vs DataSync vs Direct Storage
nodes.if_when: Need to determine whether to use Storage Gateway, DataSync, or direct S3 APIs for on-premises to cloud data movement
nodes.then: Scenario Use Legacy app needs to continue using NFS/SMB to access S3 data Storage Gateway File Gateway One-time or scheduled bulk transfer NFS/SMB → S3/EFS AWS DataSync App can use AWS SDK/CLI to write directly to S3 S3 Multipart Upload or direct SDK NAS/SAN → EBS-compatible block storage on AWS Volume Gateway or MGN

nodes.label: Purchase Option Decision — Spot vs RI vs Savings Plans vs On-Demand
nodes.if_when: Need to select the most cost-effective EC2/Fargate/RDS purchase option based on workload pattern
nodes.then: Workload Pattern Best Option Savings Stateless, interruptible, batch (ML training, transcoding, ETL) EC2 Spot / Managed Spot Training 70-90% Steady-state 24/7, long-term commitment acceptable 3-year Compute Savings Plans ~66% Steady-state 24/7, moderate commitment 1-year Compute Savings Plans ~30-40% Burst / seasonal (4-8 hrs/day) Spot (NOT RI — RI wastes idle time) 70-90% ElastiCache / RDS persistent nodes (24/7) Reserved Nodes (1-year) ~35-40% No commitment (startup, variable workload) Fargate on-demand, Aurora Serverless v2, DynamoDB on-demand Pay-per-use
never: RI/SP for burst workloads = paying for idle time. e.g. 6hr/day job on 1-year RI = 75% waste even with 40% discount. Always match commitment to utilization pattern. Savings Plans: Compute SP = EC2 + Fargate + Lambda. EC2 Instance SP = specific family. RI = specific instance type in specific AZ/region

nodes.label: RI/SP for burst workloads = paying for idle time. e.g. 6hr/day job on 1-year RI = 75% waste even with 40% discount. Always match commitment to utilization pattern. Savings Plans: Compute SP = EC2 + Fargate + Lambda. EC2 Instance SP = specific family. RI = specific instance type in specific AZ/region
S3 Storage Tier Selection — Lifecycle Rules vs Intelligent-Tiering
nodes.if_when: Need to optimize S3 storage costs based on object access patterns
nodes.then: Access Pattern Best Tier $/GB/month Accessed within 30 days S3 Standard $0.023 Accessed 30-90 days, ms retrieval needed S3 Standard-IA ~$0.0125 Accessed infrequently, ms retrieval OK S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval $0.004 Never accessed, legal hold / compliance only S3 Glacier Deep Archive $0.00099 Unknown / unpredictable access patterns S3 Intelligent-Tiering +$0.0025/1K objects monitoring fee
never: Known predictable patterns → explicit Lifecycle rules (no monitoring fee). At billions of objects the Intelligent-Tiering monitoring fee can exceed storage savings
Never-accessed data → Deep Archive (4× cheaper than Glacier IR). Paying for ms retrieval you'll never use is waste
Glacier IR minimum storage duration = 90 days. Deep Archive minimum = 180 days. Objects deleted before minimums incur early deletion fees

nodes.label: Known predictable patterns → explicit Lifecycle rules (no monitoring fee). At billions of objects the Intelligent-Tiering monitoring fee can exceed storage savings
Never-accessed data → Deep Archive (4× cheaper than Glacier IR). Paying for ms retrieval you'll never use is waste
Glacier IR minimum storage duration = 90 days. Deep Archive minimum = 180 days. Objects deleted before minimums incur early deletion fees
NAT Gateway Cost Elimination — VPC Gateway and Interface Endpoints
nodes.if_when: High NAT Gateway data processing charges from EC2 instances in private subnets accessing AWS services (S3, DynamoDB, SSM, Secrets Manager, ECR)
nodes.then: AWS Service Endpoint Type Cost S3, DynamoDB VPC Gateway Endpoint FREE — zero data processing charges SSM, Secrets Manager, ECR, KMS, STS, CloudWatch, etc. VPC Interface Endpoint (PrivateLink) $0.01/GB vs NAT $0.045/GB = 78% reduction
never: Moving EC2 to public subnets to avoid NAT — security anti-pattern, direct internet exposure
Adding NAT GW per AZ — reduces cross-AZ NAT overhead but does not eliminate S3/DynamoDB charges
Confuse Gateway Endpoints (free, S3/DynamoDB only) with Interface Endpoints (PrivateLink, hourly + data fees)
nodes.why: NAT GW = $0.045/GB. Gateway Endpoint = $0. For 100GB/day to S3 = $4.50/day = $135/month eliminated per VPC. At scale this is enormous.

nodes.label: Moving EC2 to public subnets to avoid NAT — security anti-pattern, direct internet exposure
Adding NAT GW per AZ — reduces cross-AZ NAT overhead but does not eliminate S3/DynamoDB charges
Confuse Gateway Endpoints (free, S3/DynamoDB only) with Interface Endpoints (PrivateLink, hourly + data fees)
Inter-AZ Data Transfer Cost Elimination — AZ-Aware Routing
nodes.if_when: High inter-AZ transfer costs from ECS tasks in AZ-a calling RDS/ElastiCache in AZ-b, or cross-AZ load balancer traffic
nodes.then: Enable AZ-aware routing : ECS Service Connect with AZ-affinity, or configure application to call same-AZ reader endpoint. For ALB: enable cross-zone load balancing disabled or use NLB with AZ-local DNS. Ensures callers always reach same-AZ targets.
never: Changing the database engine to fix inter-AZ costs — the charge comes from traffic crossing AZs , not the engine
Deploying all resources in a single AZ — removes HA for marginal cost saving
nodes.why: Inter-AZ data transfer = $0.01/GB each way . For high-throughput microservices this compounds rapidly. Always co-locate callers and callees in the same AZ.

nodes.label: Changing the database engine to fix inter-AZ costs — the charge comes from traffic crossing AZs , not the engine
Deploying all resources in a single AZ — removes HA for marginal cost saving
Idle Cluster Cost — Redshift Pause/Resume + Transient EMR + SageMaker Serverless
nodes.if_when: Redshift cluster idle most of the day. EMR clusters running 24/7 for 6hr/day jobs. SageMaker endpoints nearly idle (<10 req/day).
nodes.then: Service Fix Saving Redshift (4hrs active, 20hrs idle) Redshift pause/resume ~83% (pay 4/24 hrs only) EMR (6hrs active, 18hrs idle) Transient clusters (launch → run → terminate) ~75% EMR task nodes (stateless) EC2 Spot for task nodes additional 70-90% SageMaker Training (interruptible) Managed Spot Training with checkpointing 70-90% SageMaker near-idle endpoints Serverless Inference (pay per invocation, $0 idle) ~99% on near-zero traffic SageMaker idle notebooks Studio idle shutdown (built-in, stops kernel after idle period) 100% of idle cost
never: RI/SP for a cluster running 25% of the time — the discount (40%) does not offset paying for 75% idle time. Idle compute = transient/serverless, not reserved

nodes.label: RI/SP for a cluster running 25% of the time — the discount (40%) does not offset paying for 75% idle time. Idle compute = transient/serverless, not reserved
CloudWatch Logs Cost Reduction — Log Level + Export to S3
nodes.if_when: CloudWatch Logs ingestion cost is very high — application logs include DEBUG-level output in production, long retention in CloudWatch
nodes.then: Set application log level to INFO or WARN in production (DEBUG generates 10-100× more log volume → 80-90% ingestion cost reduction). Export logs older than 7-30 days to S3 ($0.023/GB/month) or S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval ($0.004/GB/month) for compliance retention. Set CloudWatch retention to 30 days.
never: Delete all CloudWatch Logs — removes operational visibility. Never set retention to <7 days for production systems needing incident investigation capability

nodes.label: Delete all CloudWatch Logs — removes operational visibility. Never set retention to <7 days for production systems needing incident investigation capability
Data Transfer Cost — CloudFront vs Direct EC2/S3 Egress
nodes.if_when: High EC2 or S3 data transfer out costs for content served directly to end users
nodes.then: Route content through CloudFront : Path $/GB (approx) Difference EC2 data transfer out $0.09 Baseline S3 data transfer out $0.09 Baseline CloudFront data transfer out $0.0085 ~90% cheaper CloudFront Origin Shield Small additional fee Reduces origin requests by 60-80% Also use CloudFront OAC (Origin Access Control) to block direct S3 URLs and force all traffic through CloudFront.

nodes.label: Fargate Right-Sizing + Aurora Serverless v2 + AZ-Local Routing
nodes.if_when: Fargate at 15% avg CPU utilization, Aurora idle 40% off-hours, S3 logs never accessed after 30 days, cross-AZ data transfer costs high — no re-architecture allowed
nodes.then: Issue Fix Saving Fargate over-provisioned (15% CPU) Right-size task definition (halve vCPU/memory) ~50% on Fargate line item Aurora idle off-hours Aurora Serverless v2 min ACU = 0.5 ~30-40% on Aurora line item S3 logs, never read after 30 days S3 Lifecycle: expire after 30 days (delete, not Glacier) 100% of post-30-day storage Cross-AZ data transfer ECS Service Connect with AZ-local routing Eliminates $0.01/GB cross-AZ charges
never: Glacier transition for data with zero value after expiry — expiry (delete) is cheaper than Glacier storage
Savings Plans on Fargate for 15% utilization — right-sizing saves more and costs nothing extra
Single-AZ deployment to eliminate cross-AZ costs — removes HA

nodes.label: Glacier transition for data with zero value after expiry — expiry (delete) is cheaper than Glacier storage
Savings Plans on Fargate for 15% utilization — right-sizing saves more and costs nothing extra
Single-AZ deployment to eliminate cross-AZ costs — removes HA
Domain 5 — Continuous Improvement for Existing Solutions
Aurora Global Database — RTO/RPO Targets + Read Latency + False-Positive Health Checks
nodes.if_when: Multi-region DR needed: RTO <15min, RPO <1min. Or cross-region read latency too high for remote users. Or Route 53 health checks triggering false failovers.
nodes.then: Need Solution RPO <1 minute (database) Aurora Global Database (storage replication ~1s lag) RPO seconds (EC2 compute) AWS Elastic Disaster Recovery (DRS) (continuous block-level) RTO <15 minutes Route 53 ARC routing controls (operator-controlled switch, no DNS TTL) Cross-region read P99 latency Read-local routing to Aurora Global DB secondary endpoint False-positive health check failovers Replace standard Route 53 health checks with ARC routing controls
never: Aurora binary log replication — higher lag, more ops than Aurora Global Database
Standard Route 53 health checks for DR — prone to false positives, TTL delay
Write forwarding to fix read latency — adds 60-90ms per write, doesn't help reads
nodes.why: Managed failover API ( failover-global-cluster ) → RTO <1 minute. Manual promotion → 1-5 min. DNS failover → 15-60 min (TTL dependent).

nodes.label: Aurora binary log replication — higher lag, more ops than Aurora Global Database
Standard Route 53 health checks for DR — prone to false positives, TTL delay
Write forwarding to fix read latency — adds 60-90ms per write, doesn't help reads
DynamoDB Hot Partitions — Write Sharding + DAX for Read Caching
nodes.if_when: DynamoDB ProvisionedThroughputExceededException on specific partition keys (popular SKUs, viral items). High RCU cost from repeated hot-item reads.
nodes.then: Problem Fix Hot write partitions Write sharding : append random suffix to partition key ( SKU-001-shard-3 ). Scatter writes across N partitions. Scatter-gather on read. Hot read partitions + high RCU cost DynamoDB DAX (in-memory cluster, microsecond reads, drop-in DynamoDB API replacement, caches hot items)
never: DynamoDB auto-scaling — 5-15 min reaction time, doesn't fix per-partition throttling during spikes
DynamoDB on-demand capacity — still throttles at per-partition limits (3K RCU / 1K WCU per partition)
ElastiCache Redis instead of DAX — Redis requires application code changes; DAX is a drop-in DynamoDB API replacement
nodes.why: DynamoDB per-partition limits: 3K RCU / 1K WCU regardless of table capacity mode. Only sharding distributes writes across multiple partitions. Table-level capacity doesn't help hot partitions.

nodes.label: DynamoDB auto-scaling — 5-15 min reaction time, doesn't fix per-partition throttling during spikes
DynamoDB on-demand capacity — still throttles at per-partition limits (3K RCU / 1K WCU per partition)
ElastiCache Redis instead of DAX — Redis requires application code changes; DAX is a drop-in DynamoDB API replacement
Lambda Cold Start — SnapStart (Java) vs Provisioned Concurrency
nodes.if_when: Lambda cold start >2s on Java runtime (Spring Boot). Or any runtime with cold start latency causing P99 SLA violations.
nodes.then: Solution How Cost Best For Lambda SnapStart Snapshots initialized execution environment after init phase. Restores from snapshot on cold start. No additional cost during idle Java 11/17/21 only. Cold start drops to <1s. Provisioned Concurrency Pre-warms N instances, always warm, zero cold start Pay for pre-warmed instances always Any runtime. Guaranteed zero cold start when concurrency is within provisioned capacity.
never: Increasing Lambda memory to fix cold starts — memory reduces execution duration, NOT initialization time (Spring Boot startup = I/O bound, not CPU bound)
Rewriting in Python/Node.js — valid but SnapStart is less invasive. Python cold starts can also be significant for large functions

nodes.label: Increasing Lambda memory to fix cold starts — memory reduces execution duration, NOT initialization time (Spring Boot startup = I/O bound, not CPU bound)
Rewriting in Python/Node.js — valid but SnapStart is less invasive. Python cold starts can also be significant for large functions
SQS DLQ — Root Cause vs Symptom Fix + Message Schema Validation
nodes.if_when: SQS DLQ accumulating thousands of messages — Lambda consumer failing on malformed payloads or processing errors
nodes.then: Fix the consumer logic: add schema validation → on valid: process + explicit DeleteMessage . On invalid payload: log to S3 quarantine + explicit DeleteMessage (don't re-enqueue). On genuine processing failure: let message return to queue and DLQ after maxReceiveCount . Then replay existing DLQ messages with corrected consumer.
never: Replay DLQ without fixing the consumer — same malformed messages go straight back to DLQ
Increasing visibility timeout — does not fix malformed message handling, just delays DLQ entry
Deleting DLQ messages without investigation — loss of debugging data
nodes.why: SQS Lambda trigger: Lambda must call DeleteMessage on success. Without explicit delete, the message returns to queue after visibility timeout. DLQ receives messages after maxReceiveCount processing attempts.

nodes.label: Replay DLQ without fixing the consumer — same malformed messages go straight back to DLQ
Increasing visibility timeout — does not fix malformed message handling, just delays DLQ entry
Deleting DLQ messages without investigation — loss of debugging data
EKS/ECS Observability — ADOT Tracing + App Mesh Circuit Breaker + Argo Rollouts Canary
nodes.if_when: Cannot trace requests across microservices. Slow downstream service causing cascading failures. No automated canary rollback with <5% blast radius.
nodes.then: Problem Solution Cross-service distributed tracing AWS Distro for OpenTelemetry (ADOT) DaemonSet → X-Ray (trace ID propagated via X-Amzn-Trace-Id header through all 12 services) Cascading failure from slow upstream App Mesh Envoy outlierDetection (passive circuit breaker — ejects failing upstream from pool, no app code changes) Canary with automated rollback Argo Rollouts + CloudWatch Analysis Template (auto-rollback on error rate threshold at 5% traffic)
never: CodeDeploy for EKS Fargate pod-level canary — CodeDeploy integrates with EC2/Lambda, not Kubernetes Deployment objects
K8s liveness probes as circuit breakers — probes restart pods, don't prevent thread pool exhaustion from slow upstream
Jaeger (self-managed) when ADOT is available — adds operational overhead

nodes.label: CodeDeploy for EKS Fargate pod-level canary — CodeDeploy integrates with EC2/Lambda, not Kubernetes Deployment objects
K8s liveness probes as circuit breakers — probes restart pods, don't prevent thread pool exhaustion from slow upstream
Jaeger (self-managed) when ADOT is available — adds operational overhead
Kinesis Hot Shards — Partition Key Cardinality + Lambda Iterator Age
nodes.if_when: Kinesis shards unbalanced (3/20 shards get 80% of records) due to low-cardinality partition key. Lambda iterator age rising (>15 min) due to slow processing.
nodes.then: Problem Fix Hot write shards (low-cardinality partition key) Change producer partition key to high-cardinality value (e.g. customer_id , record_uuid ) — uniform hash distribution across all shards Lambda iterator age >15min (slow HTTP enrichment call) Decouple enrichment: Lambda writes raw record to S3/SQS immediately (<5ms), separate async process calls enrichment API
never: Increasing shard count for low-cardinality key — 4 values on 40 shards = still only 4 shards get traffic
Kinesis Enhanced Fan-Out for hot shards — EFO increases read throughput per consumer, does NOT fix hot write shard imbalance
nodes.why: Low cardinality partition key (e.g. 4 values) → at most 4 shard hash buckets active → remaining shards idle. Fix is always at the producer partition key .

nodes.label: Increasing shard count for low-cardinality key — 4 values on 40 shards = still only 4 shards get traffic
Kinesis Enhanced Fan-Out for hot shards — EFO increases read throughput per consumer, does NOT fix hot write shard imbalance
RDS Performance — Performance Insights + Read Replicas + VACUUM + Replica Lag
nodes.if_when: RDS/Aurora CPU 90% at peak, query performance degraded 40% over months, read replica lagging 45s during peak, no maintenance performed
nodes.then: Problem Fix High writer CPU, unknown query bottleneck RDS Performance Insights (DB load per SQL, wait events, top SQL ranked by impact) Offload reads from writer Add RDS/Aurora Read Replica , route SELECT queries to replica endpoint Table bloat + stale planner stats Redshift Automatic Table Optimization (ATO) or enable Aurora automatic VACUUM/ANALYZE Replica lag 45s during peak Route latency-sensitive reads to writer, or upgrade replica instance class, or reduce write transaction size

nodes.label: Lambda Canary + Weighted Aliases + CodeDeploy Automated Rollback
nodes.if_when: Lambda deployed directly without staged rollout — production incidents from bad deployments, need automated canary with <5% blast radius and auto-rollback
nodes.then: Lambda weighted aliases : aws lambda update-alias --routing-config AdditionalVersionWeights={"2":0.10} → 10% traffic to new version. CodeDeploy with LambdaCanary10Percent5Minutes deployment config automates traffic shift + monitors CloudWatch alarm → auto-rolls back if error rate exceeds threshold.
never: Deploy Lambda directly to production without an alias — no canary capability, immediate 100% blast radius
CodeDeploy LINEAR_10PERCENT_EVERY_1MINUTE for EKS pod-level canary — CodeDeploy is for EC2/Lambda, not Kubernetes

nodes.label: Deploy Lambda directly to production without an alias — no canary capability, immediate 100% blast radius
CodeDeploy LINEAR_10PERCENT_EVERY_1MINUTE for EKS pod-level canary — CodeDeploy is for EC2/Lambda, not Kubernetes
Technical Debt Remediation — Drift, IAM Over-Privilege, Logs, S3 Encryption, EOL OS
nodes.if_when: 500 CloudFormation stacks with drift, 2,400 over-privileged IAM roles, no centralized logging, inconsistent S3 encryption, EOL EC2 OS with CVEs
nodes.then: Problem AWS-Native Fix CloudFormation drift across 20 accounts Config conformance pack + CLOUDFORMATION_STACK_DRIFT_DETECTION_CHECK Over-privileged IAM roles (2,400 roles) IAM Access Analyzer policy generation from CloudTrail (least-privilege based on actual usage history) No centralized logs (7-day per-account) CloudTrail Organization Trail + S3 Object Lock COMPLIANCE + CloudTrail Lake for SQL queries Inconsistent S3 encryption SCP: Deny s3:PutObject unless s3:x-amz-server-side-encryption = aws:kms EOL OS + CVEs Amazon Inspector (CVE scanning org-wide) → Security Hub + SSM Patch Manager
never: AWS Config for CloudFormation stack drift — Config tracks resource configuration, not CF stack drift (requires detect-stack-drift API)
GuardDuty for over-privileged IAM — GuardDuty = runtime anomaly detection, not static policy analysis
CloudWatch Logs Insights for cross-account queries when logs are in per-account S3 — logs must first be in CloudWatch Logs

nodes.label: AWS Config for CloudFormation stack drift — Config tracks resource configuration, not CF stack drift (requires detect-stack-drift API)
GuardDuty for over-privileged IAM — GuardDuty = runtime anomaly detection, not static policy analysis
CloudWatch Logs Insights for cross-account queries when logs are in per-account S3 — logs must first be in CloudWatch Logs
Serverless Burst Handling — SQS Buffer + EventBridge Decoupling + PITR
nodes.if_when: API Gateway 429s during traffic burst. EventBridge consumer overwhelmed (DLQ backlog). DynamoDB PITR disabled (accidental delete = hours of recovery). Lambda no staged rollout.
nodes.then: Issue Fix API GW burst → 429s Add SQS queue in front (API GW → Lambda → SQS enqueue → 200 OK immediately; processing decoupled) Slow EventBridge consumer (DLQ backlog) Add SQS queue between EventBridge and Lambda consumer (buffer, AZ-resilient, Standard SQS not FIFO) DynamoDB PITR disabled Enable DynamoDB PITR (restore to any second in last 35 days — 1-click recovery) No Lambda canary Lambda weighted alias + CodeDeploy + CloudWatch alarm auto-rollback
never: SQS FIFO for high-throughput EventBridge fan-out — FIFO max 3K msg/sec. Use Standard SQS (unlimited throughput) for high-volume buffering

nodes.label: SQS FIFO for high-throughput EventBridge fan-out — FIFO max 3K msg/sec. Use Standard SQS (unlimited throughput) for high-volume buffering
CI/CD Improvements — Blue/Green, Build Cache, Secrets, Rollback
nodes.if_when: CodeDeploy in-place causing downtime. Slow builds. Secrets hardcoded. Manual rollback takes hours.
nodes.then: Problem Fix Deployment downtime CodeDeploy Blue/Green with ALB traffic shifting (zero-downtime, instant rollback) Slow builds CodeBuild caching (S3 or local cache for dependencies) + ARM-based builds (Graviton = 20% cheaper) No automated testing gate CodePipeline Test stage → CodeBuild action → fail pipeline on test failure before deploy Secrets in buildspec.yml AWS Secrets Manager referenced as CodeBuild environment variable (auto-injected at runtime) Slow rollback Blue/Green: shift ALB weight back to Blue = <60 seconds
never: SSM Parameter Store for secrets needing automatic rotation — Parameter Store has no built-in rotation Lambda. Secrets Manager has built-in rotation for RDS, Redshift, DocumentDB

nodes.label: SSM Parameter Store for secrets needing automatic rotation — Parameter Store has no built-in rotation Lambda. Secrets Manager has built-in rotation for RDS, Redshift, DocumentDB
Well-Architected DR — RTO/RPO + WAF + mTLS + Secrets Rotation + Chaos Engineering
nodes.if_when: Well-Architected review gaps: 8hr RTO / 4hr RPO, no WAF, 14-month-old secrets, HTTP internal traffic, no chaos testing
nodes.then: Gap Fix RTO 8hr → <15min DRS (EC2 continuous replication) + Aurora Global Database + Route 53 ARC routing controls No WAF (SQL injection in logs) AWS WAF + AWS Managed Rule Groups ( AWSManagedRulesSQLiRuleSet , CommonRuleSet ) on API Gateway Manual secret rotation (14 months) Secrets Manager automatic rotation (built-in Lambda for RDS — dual-password, zero downtime) HTTP internal VPC traffic ACM Private CA + App Mesh mTLS (encrypts service-to-service traffic, no app code changes) No chaos testing AWS Fault Injection Simulator (FIS) — managed experiments (AZ failure, network latency, instance termination) with safety guardrails
never: Shield Advanced as WAF — Shield = volumetric DDoS protection. SQLi = application-layer → AWS WAF
Parameter Store for automatic rotation — only Secrets Manager has built-in rotation Lambda
CloudWatch alarms as chaos engineering — alarms react to failures, they don't inject failures

nodes.label: Shield Advanced as WAF — Shield = volumetric DDoS protection. SQLi = application-layer → AWS WAF
Parameter Store for automatic rotation — only Secrets Manager has built-in rotation Lambda
CloudWatch alarms as chaos engineering — alarms react to failures, they don't inject failures
Glue ETL Data Quality + Pipeline Reliability Patterns
nodes.if_when: Glue ETL jobs failing on malformed source files. Bad data reaching Redshift corrupting dashboards. No data quality checks between pipeline stages.
nodes.then: Problem Fix Glue re-processing already-ingested files Glue job bookmarks — tracks already-processed S3 objects, skips them on next run Malformed files causing job failures Pre-validation Lambda/Glue Python shell → quarantine malformed files to S3 error prefix → SNS alert upstream team No data quality gates AWS Glue Data Quality — DQ rules (completeness, referential integrity, range) evaluated inline during ETL. Failed records → quarantine before Redshift. Redshift table bloat / stale stats Redshift Automatic Table Optimization (ATO) — background VACUUM + ANALYZE automatically
